Alright, step number 14 is reverse all risk. This positions the explicit classic guarantees that most people are used to using or used to seeing versus a deeper knowledge of risk reversal which is a little bit different than just guaranteeing something. In fact I have people in my life, I don't know if you do, who just say, you know, the Vikings are going to win next week, I guarantee it. And it's like really what are you going to do if they don't win? Well I guarantee they will. Well no you don't. You're just using it as a word to mean that you really think they're going to win. A real guarantee has some teeth to it. And the deep knowledge of risk reversal means you understand what it means when you say you're going to guarantee something. As a marketer you can't use the word guarantee capriciously. You have to understand what it means and how to use it correctly. So you have to understand the objections that you're overcoming with a guarantee. It's the fear of getting screwed, the fear of being made a fool, or of facing your spouse and lost neighbors, that whole thing I talk about a lot, if you've made a bad deal. You know it's one thing to make a bad deal to buy something that turns out to be not any good or falls apart or something. It's one thing for you to face yourself with like, wow I made a really bad purchase there. It's quite another to have other people in your life use that as a bludgeon over your head to remind you of how, what an idiot you are. That does happen. So this is one of the main objections that people have. They're thinking, I don't know, I want it, I could probably live with myself if it wasn't a good deal, but I could never live it down if Suzy or Joe Nextdoor or my damn brother-in-law found out about this. So you've got to address those objections. You also need to know where any kind of guarantee goes in your funnel. A lot of people think that if they put the guarantee at the very beginning that that's where it belongs because you're addressing a big objection. Just because it's a big objection doesn't mean that that's the first objection you deal with. You have to go through some other steps, getting people involved, pulling them into the ad, getting them pretty far into the process before you start meeting those major objections. Because the objections don't even come up until they started to think about buying. So it's not headline worthy in most cases. This is to remove one obstacle, the fear of getting taken or the fear of making a mistake, basically. So you want to be bold with your guarantees, but you want to be aware of the logistics of boldness in higher refunds, more moochers coming in to take your stuff and then not buying or refunding afterwards just because that's how they operate. Higher levels of customer service required. If you have a guarantee where you can use this for a year and we'll help you and we'll do everything at the end of the year, if you're not completely happy then just send it back and you'll get 100% of your money back. That's actually a guarantee that people have made. However, you've got to be aware that this means refunds may go up a year from now and your customer services may be overwhelmed trying to keep people happy throughout the year as they look for reasons to be able to return the thing. However, if you understand all this, if this is part of your corporate culture and you understand how to make that work, then go for it. The unconditional guarantee versus the conditional guarantee. The classic one you see is the unconditional guarantee. I guarantee 100% that this will work or you get 100% of your money back. No questions asked. You don't need to do anything. Just tell me you're unhappy and you get all of your money back. There may be no time limit. It may just be or there may be a three month, six month, a year time limit, whatever, but it's pretty much unconditional. You don't have to tell me why you weren't happy. Then the conditional guarantee is something sometimes people use. For example, if you have a product where you're going to invest a lot of time, energy or money yourself while they have a period of time where they can decide if they're going to get asked for a refund or not. If you're going to do that, it's fair to ask for a conditional refund. It's not unconditional. It's conditional. You have to show me that you went through three of the steps. You have to make three of the phone calls that we make or you have to call me back. One that used to be used was for some clients that I knew about that had a business proposition. They were going to help you get your business going off, help your business get off the ground. One of the steps was to make your business card. The conditional guarantee was you can get your money back in any time of the first year, but send us a copy of the business card that you made because that was one of the early steps that they had. This actually cut down refunds dramatically because it turns out a lot of the people I were asking for refunds weren't doing anything at all. They were, and yet there was a lot of time and energy and materials invested in helping them try to get something going. If they weren't even going to make the step of going to get business cards made, which was simple, cheap, and easy, then they weren't in a position to get their money back. Quite fair to do that. Be honest with any caveats. Caveats are something that you use where it's say, there's one condition I have to this guarantee, but it's very rational and it's very simple and I think you'll agree with me once you understand what it is. You say, I've been in your shoes before and I hate trusting someone and getting taken. I'm going to give you the largest guarantee that I'm capable of making. However, because I also know that sometimes people will refund without thinking it through, I want to make sure that you've at least tried something. Please make sure that you've done the first lesson in this or whatever it is and send me just proof that you actually did something. That's very easy to pull off. If you've gotten to the point where you're squabbling over money, show him how he doesn't risk a penny. This is one of the more common guarantees. Even though you're paying this, you're going to get 100% of your money back. Sometimes we say you'll get 200% of your money back if you meet certain conditions. But you want to make sure, what's more important than him making more money back is that he doesn't risk a penny. Because the idea that you will risk money at all, even a little bit, can bring up an objection that will stop the sales process cold. Bonuses that you give out, and that's a good way to make sure that any idea that I'm risking anything, if I'm getting a big pile of stuff, then that sometimes reduces the risk. That reduces the sense that I'm not sure what the value is that I'm getting. Bonuses should equal or exceed the value of the entire deal, or be so desired as to drive the sale. Often when I wrote a lot for the financial newsletter industry, and often we wouldn't even bring up the fact that to get this bonus, this bonus report that we're talking about, you have to become a member and get the newsletter for one year. We don't mention that. The first part of the ad is all about the bonus, because the bonuses were great. Here's how to get this free report and a free book on what to do when the Dow hits 2000. There's a free report on how to make a mutual funds pay off for you and all this stuff. It sounds great so that when you get to the point that how do I get this, I pay $19 a year and I get this financial newsletter. It's like, wow, I really want this. It reverses the whole sense of that if I just said $19 and I'll give you a newsletter for the rest of the year and do you want it or not? They'll say, nah, even $20, I don't want it. I just don't want it. But if you make those bonuses so desirable, that can overcome any of the resistance. Rodale, the company that I mentioned a few times, had one of the best refunds policies for their books, for their book division that I had ever seen. It was pretty standard. You can see the book for 21 days for free. At the end of 21 days, you'll be billed. However, you can send it back at any time and get your money back. In fact, they said there was a lifetime refund. Now, a lot of the writers before me didn't really play that up very much, but I asked them to clarify, wait a minute. I get 21 days to see this before I get a bill. If I send it back before then, then you can cancel the bill and there's no problem at all. However, even if I get the bill and I pay and later on I decide I don't want this book, I can get a refund. They said, yes, absolutely. We guarantee everything we have unconditionally. I said, what about my kids? Can they get a refund? Yes, you absolutely can. And so I put in my copy. Not only do you have a lifetime refund, but your kids and your kids' kids can send the book back later and get a full refund from Rodale. That was kind of funny. People chuckled at that, but that was their policy and nobody had really made it manifest before. So it's kind of a numbers game when you're talking about doing your guarantee. What's it worth to your bottom line to bring in more people at a higher risk of refunds? In other words, if you make a certain offer and you have a certain kind of refund, so it's a certain price to get in and you can get all your money back with no questions asked and you don't even need to send the product back, for example, that may bring more people in. Even if you say, well, you get to keep all these bonuses, plus you can keep the product and just tell me, excuse me, tell me you're unhappy and we will send you all your money back or just tell us at any time you're unhappy. That will bring more people into the mix. It will also raise the number of refunds you have to deal with. So what is it worth to your bottom line at the end of the day to have more people come in to your offer, but also have more people refund? And believe it or not, in a lot of situations, the higher your refund rate, the more profit you have at the end of the day because that higher rate also corresponds to a lot more people taking you up on your offer. However, you've got to understand how this works and it comes down to math. Give people a metric to measure how happy they are. If you haven't doubled sales or if you haven't made $50,000 in the first two weeks of trying this, or if you haven't lost five pounds and kept it off for three weeks, or if you aren't meeting more people out in the world, if you haven't got five more clients by the end of next month, just give them a metric to help them measure. And just say, if you haven't reached this metric, then by God, you can have a refund. Because that helps people think before they buy, they say, that's pretty, you know, I would be really happy with five extra clients at the end of two months. So I will try this. And at the end of the two months, they may have had those five, they may have had ten extra clients, may have only had three, but they knew they weren't applying everything. And in reality, they thought three does it for me too, although it wouldn't have helped in the original ad, it wouldn't have flipped them the buy. But after they've had the product and they've seen that it works, three actually did the job of five. Appreciate the customers who stay and buy, and those are the people you want in your, on your house list and in your world. Forgive, but forget those who refund and leave. That's culling the list. Sometimes marketers will give people a couple of chances. And if you have somebody who's bought from you several times, and then they make a refund, check into it, have your customer service check into it, and maybe they were generally dissatisfied. Maybe that information is something you should know that they're doing you a favor. It's like, wow, here's a guy who bought and liked and kept a lot of stuff. Now he's refunded. Don't get rid of him right away. Take that, take that into account when you're recreating the product or doing the next sale. And actually give him a deal if he wants. A good customer or wants to buy over and over again. And sometimes they will refund too. There are a number of people out there who by knowing they're going to refund later. And if you can track it and you find out that people are refunding, then you can't get rid of them because they'll boost your initial profit estimates on sales, but they will damage it at the end because they will increase the amount of refunds. So use guarantees to position yourself against the competition. Find out what the competition is offering, and then use your guarantee to make yourself either better, more generous, more exacting, have a longer guarantee, change it up. But know what your competition is looking at because your prospect probably knows or will find out. And so if you have a less generous guarantee, that's not a good thing. So the classic guarantee, and I'm just going to read this to you. This is an example of a classic unconditional guarantee. Six month, 100% money back unconditional guarantee. Order this package without fear and check out all the secrets and shortcuts for six full months. If you're not blown away by what you discover, simply send the package back in any condition and we'll refund your $97 immediately. No questions asked. We've been in business in the US for over 15 years and our reputation is based on being straight and honest with our customers. We treat you the way we wish other businesses would treat us. With immediate shipping, strict courtesy, the customer is always right, and prompt refunds when requested. This unconditional guarantee means you can see everything for free if you want. You don't risk anything by ordering now. However, you must hurry. We've only duped a small number of these DVDs and have no plans to create anymore. This is essentially a market test. If you want to discover how to break into the pro ranks of the best basis in the world, you must respond today. This was an ad for DVDs on how to play a professional level bass. Add a little pizzazz. This happens later in, I believe, the same ad. Don't believe me? Fine. Here's my personal guarantee. Check it out for yourself. If you don't agree, oh, this is a different product, check it out for yourself. If you don't agree this is the most vicious and simple to master fighting system you've ever experienced, then I'll give you $10 from my own pocket. But you must act fast because once the military learns it allowed this stuff to leak into civilian hands, you will never hear about it again. Plus, I've arranged so you can see everything for free if you choose. Plus $10. That's adding a little pizzazz. Now explain the deal in juicy turns. Here's what I've set up for you. To join the Special Operations Club, all you have to do is say yes to my invitation here. You do not pay a dime of front and there are no membership fees ever. The only way anyone can join is to be asked personally by me. I will immediately send you this kit. It's yours to keep free and clear no matter what. And then it goes on. So these are good examples of taking the basic classic guarantee of, hey, buy it and if you're not happy I'll give you your money back and spreading it out a little bit. So you're really dealing with the element of risk that your prospect feels. He's going to put his money on the table, yes, but also his self-respect, his ego and his hopes and dreams and a lot of other things are going into this. So respect your guarantee, but put it in the right place, which is right around the close where he's going to lay money down. Reversing the risk. Hundreds, thousands of questions over the years. I think almost all of them, either you've already answered them in the lecture or there's a couple things I'd like to go a little deeper on that have been on the same theme. You said why you don't open up with a guarantee. There's always someone who comes back with the first exercise they submit or the first question is I want to put the guarantee in the headline. Explain a little bit more why that's not frequently effective. Because it's not the objection that comes up right away. When people are thinking about the product, when you're explaining things to them, their objections are I don't know, this isn't really for me, I'm not worthy of this or this is over my head or geez you're going to tell me I got to work really hard at doing this. All of these other objections are tumbling up. When you do the guarantee right off the bat and sometimes we will throw the guarantee in at the start, not necessarily in the headline but maybe in the superscript or in the subhead, only to brush up against that particular objection. So when you're able to just knock off multiple objections really quick, then sure, sometimes you want to throw that in and you get it for free or and you get a free look and you're not going to spend a penny to find any of this out. So that's fine but that's not explaining the guarantee. And when you get to the guarantee, it's actually you spell it out, you want to take the time to say let me explain to you why you don't risk a single thing. So it's just the order that you come up with the objections. And again it's like that one-to-one conversation with you and your prospect. It's when are the objections coming up and you want to be able to time it exactly. So when you're talking about the money, that's the time to raise that objection in their mind, that was the time to lessen their concern about being out the money, making a poor buying decision. Okay, the other question we always get from a lot of people and you may still have the question but hopefully we can help you out here, at least cut one iteration with your coach out. How long should the guarantee be? Why if you mentioned people already, let's just start with a one-year guarantee. If it's for, what are you really risking with that? Well, one of the things is, one of the classic ways to look at it is that if you have a very short guarantee, then it kind of remains in the mind of the prospect or the customer once he's got it. It's like you'll think the package arrives or he gets it online or whatever, he's saying wow, okay, I've got to get right to this because I only got a week or I have 24 hours to decide. And it just doesn't seem like enough time. And you're thinking, that's an objection where I can say, that's not enough time. I don't think I can make a good decision. So why even bother getting it because I'll just have to send it back anyway. Lengthening that out, 30 days is certainly a common one and a lot of your competition may have 30 days or 60 days because those are a month long approximately. And a month sounds like a lot more time to be able to do it, maybe. But that's why if you want to get beyond that a year, one of the good things about a year is that people feel like they have oodles of time to be able to get involved in this. It doesn't just counter the objection that I'm risking all this money to try to do this. And when I say all this money, it could be a $19 product or a $19,000 product. The idea of being taken or the idea that you made a bad investment will start to wear on them. And then it just starts to air everything out. That paranoia that you're stuck with this starts to go away and starts to vanish. And the longer you go and as some people have had, they have a lifetime guarantee because they know over a period of time by tracking this that the lifetime guarantee just allows people room to breathe and they don't feel the urgency of needing to decide, well, either one or I don't, I'm going to send it back. And so refunds actually go down the longer your guarantee is in many cases. Yeah. I would just say that that was not in every case, but if your cost of delivery is a book or it's a DVD or if it's digital. So really your increment, each time you deliver this product, it's not what is that cost. You're simply, whether it's a $9 product, $19,99, $1900 product, the planet is not populated with people who are, oh, I'm going to buy this for the sake of counting 364 days and returning it the last day. They still have to take action to return. So it's simply not going, like in the lecture you expressed some concern of the downside of the one year guarantee. A lot of clients over a lot of years now, exactly zero have come back to me a year later and said, oh yeah, that was, we got that rush of people who remembered that a year ago they ordered that book or that course. They kept it right on top of the desk. So after they consumed everything for $12 or $11. It just doesn't happen. Yeah, exactly. It's just like you don't, you don't do that with anything, but for 30 days it might stay on top of your desk. If you can risk going longer, it's buried. If you've even if you're, it's under, it's now a piece of furniture as I call it. There's something else stacked on top of it. If it's a physical product, it's somewhere on a computer hard drive, they may not have the computer anymore. So again, there's some offset to that. If your cost of delivery is high, then there are other business reasons for shortening your guarantee period. But it is like- Or if you invest more, if you have to put more energy or you actually, if you're hosting phone calls or podcasts. Thank you because the cost includes human time, whether it's your paying your employees for the time or you yourself are delivering. There's very few consulting, credible consulting businesses that can afford to say, I will do everything in my power to help you. Well, here's the plan, let's get the plan. And if you don't succeed in a month or a week, I'll give you all the money back. It's just that is you, that the human capital has been invested. Whether it's a book or a course, you have a lot more reality that your cash flow, your business structure, lets you to be more generous with the guarantee. Yeah, makes sense. Okay. We've got a couple more sections to go here. Oh yeah, we're rocking and rolling now. Let John explain how the exercise is going to work and then we'll move on to the next step. All right, now it's your turn to try crafting your best possible guarantee or risk reversal option. Think about all the objections that you've been listing in prior lessons and how you counter them and put yourself in the place of your prospect. What is it that's holding them up that if you could just say, nope, it's going to be okay that he will finally decide to buy, that he'll be more comfortable putting money down for this? So is it his discomfort at the amount of money out? Does he feel like he's going to get taken? Does he feel like he needs more time to think about it? Does he feel that he's not qualified for this? What are the things that you can address in a very simple and straightforward guarantee that will help him decide, yes. I do not feel like I'm putting any money or myself at risk by doing this. So give it a shot.
